title: Planning a Community Garden
summary: Rosa and Ben discuss their plans to convert an unused piece of land in their neighbourhood into a shared community garden, covering design, funding, and how to get residents involved.

body.speaker: Rosa
body.text: Ben, have you had a chance to look at the empty lot on Maple Street? I've been thinking about it a lot lately.

body.speaker: Ben
body.text: Yeah, I walked past it yesterday. It's just sitting there with weeds and rubbish. What are you thinking?

body.speaker: Rosa
body.text: I want to turn it into a community garden. I've already spoken to the council, and they're open to the idea as long as we present a proper proposal.

body.speaker: Ben
body.text: That sounds fantastic. I've always wanted to get involved in something like this. Where would we even start?

body.speaker: Rosa
body.text: I think we need to start by surveying the land to see what condition it's in. We also need to know if the soil is suitable for growing vegetables, or if we'll need to bring in fresh topsoil.

body.speaker: Ben
body.text: Good point. We should probably also think about what kinds of things the community would actually want to grow. Not everyone is interested in vegetables — some people might prefer flowers or herbs.

body.speaker: Rosa
body.text: Exactly. That's why I'd like to hold a neighbourhood meeting first. If we get residents involved from the very beginning, they'll feel a sense of ownership and be more likely to take care of the space.

body.speaker: Ben
body.text: That makes sense. Have you thought about funding? A project like this is going to cost money — tools, seeds, fencing, maybe a water connection.

body.speaker: Rosa
body.text: I've been researching grants that are available for community projects. There's one from the local council that covers up to two thousand dollars for green spaces. There's also a national environmental charity that funds urban gardening initiatives.

body.speaker: Ben
body.text: Two thousand dollars might not be enough, though. Do you think we could also ask local businesses to sponsor us? I know a few shop owners on the high street who care about the neighbourhood.

body.speaker: Rosa
body.text: That's a brilliant idea. We could offer to put their names on a sign at the entrance — it would be good publicity for them and help us reach our budget at the same time.

body.speaker: Ben
body.text: What about the design of the garden itself? Should we have individual plots that people rent, or one big shared growing space?

body.speaker: Rosa
body.text: I'd prefer a mix of both. Individual plots give people a personal space where they can grow whatever they like, but a shared area means we can grow food for the whole community, including people who don't have time to maintain their own plot.

body.speaker: Ben
body.text: I like that idea. We could even set aside a section for children, with easy crops like sunflowers and strawberries. Getting kids involved early is really important.

body.speaker: Rosa
body.text: Absolutely. And if we run workshops on composting and organic growing, we can educate people while building the garden at the same time. It becomes more than just a place to grow food — it becomes a learning space.

body.speaker: Ben
body.text: This is really exciting. When are you thinking of presenting the proposal to the council?

body.speaker: Rosa
body.text: I'd like to have everything ready within the next six weeks. If we get approval in time, we could start clearing the land by early spring and have the garden ready to plant before summer.

body.speaker: Ben
body.text: Count me in. I'll help with the survey this weekend if the weather holds. Let's make this happen.

annotations.meaningVi: lô đất trống, mảnh đất bỏ hoang
annotations.exampleEn: They turned the empty lot into a playground for local children.

annotations.meaningVi: sẵn lòng xem xét, không phản đối
annotations.exampleEn: The manager said she was open to the idea of flexible working hours.

annotations.meaningVi: đề xuất chính thức, tờ trình hợp lệ
annotations.exampleEn: You'll need to submit a proper proposal before the committee will consider it.

annotations.meaningVi: khảo sát địa điểm, kiểm tra mảnh đất
annotations.exampleEn: Before building, they spent a week surveying the land.

annotations.meaningVi: đất mặt, lớp đất trên cùng
annotations.exampleEn: We had to bring in fresh topsoil because the garden had been paved over.

annotations.meaningVi: cảm giác làm chủ, tinh thần trách nhiệm
annotations.exampleEn: Giving people a say in decisions creates a sense of ownership.
She applied for a grant to fund her research project.

annotations.meaningVi: sáng kiến làm vườn đô thị
annotations.exampleEn: Urban gardening initiatives have been growing in popularity across major cities.
A local business agreed to sponsor the community event.

annotations.meaningVi: quảng bá tốt, hình ảnh tốt
annotations.exampleEn: Donating to the charity gave the company good publicity.

annotations.meaningVi: ô đất riêng lẻ, mảnh đất cá nhân
annotations.exampleEn: Each family was given one of the individual plots to maintain.
They set aside a corner of the park for children's activities.

annotations.meaningVi: ủ phân hữu cơ, làm phân compost
annotations.exampleEn: Composting kitchen waste reduces the amount sent to landfill.

annotations.meaningVi: trồng trọt hữu cơ, canh tác không dùng hóa chất
annotations.exampleEn: They are committed to organic growing and use no pesticides.

annotations.meaningVi: nếu thời tiết vẫn tốt, nếu trời không mưa
annotations.exampleEn: We'll have the picnic outside if the weather holds.

annotations.meaningVi: tính tôi vào, tôi tham gia
annotations.exampleEn: If you're going hiking this weekend, count me in.

grammarNotes.title: First Conditional — Planning and real future possibilities
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Rosa và Ben dùng điều kiện loại 1 nhiều lần để nói về các kế hoạch trong tương lai. Ví dụ: 'If we get approval in time, we could start clearing the land by early spring.' Cấu trúc: If + hiện tại đơn, will/can/could + động từ nguyên mẫu. 'Could' thay cho 'will' để thể hiện khả năng, không phải sự chắc chắn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: First conditionals are used throughout to discuss real future plans. 'If we get residents involved from the very beginning, they'll feel a sense of ownership.' Structure: If + present simple, will/can/could + bare infinitive. Using 'could' instead of 'will' softens the certainty, suggesting a likely but not guaranteed outcome.

grammarNotes.title: Modal Verbs — Suggestions, obligations, and possibilities
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng nhiều động từ khuyết thiếu để đề xuất ý kiến và bàn luận về kế hoạch. 'We should probably think about...' (lời khuyên nhẹ nhàng), 'We could offer to put their names on a sign' (đề nghị), 'we can educate people' (khả năng). Mỗi động từ khuyết thiếu mang sắc thái khác nhau: should = lời khuyên, could = đề nghị/khả năng, can = khả năng chung.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Modal verbs express different degrees of certainty and suggestion. 'We should probably think about...' = mild advice. 'We could offer to put their names on a sign' = suggestion or possibility. 'We can educate people' = general possibility. 'Should' is stronger than 'could', and both are softer than 'must' or 'have to'.

translationVi:
Rosa: Ben, bạn có cơ hội nhìn vào mảnh đất trống trên phố Maple chưa? Gần đây tôi đã suy nghĩ rất nhiều về nó.
Ben: Có, tôi đi ngang qua đó hôm qua. Nó chỉ nằm đó với cỏ dại và rác. Bạn đang nghĩ gì vậy?
Rosa: Tôi muốn biến nó thành một khu vườn cộng đồng. Tôi đã nói chuyện với hội đồng địa phương rồi, và họ sẵn lòng xem xét ý tưởng miễn là chúng ta trình bày một đề xuất đúng đắn.
Ben: Nghe tuyệt quá. Tôi luôn muốn tham gia vào điều gì đó như thế này. Chúng ta bắt đầu từ đâu?
Rosa: Tôi nghĩ chúng ta cần bắt đầu bằng cách khảo sát mảnh đất để xem tình trạng của nó. Chúng ta cũng cần biết đất có phù hợp để trồng rau không, hoặc liệu chúng ta có cần đưa vào đất mặt mới.
Ben: Điểm hay đấy. Chúng ta cũng nên nghĩ về những thứ mà cộng đồng thực sự muốn trồng. Không phải ai cũng quan tâm đến rau — một số người có thể thích hoa hoặc thảo mộc hơn.
Rosa: Chính xác. Đó là lý do tôi muốn tổ chức cuộc họp khu phố trước. Nếu chúng ta thu hút cư dân tham gia từ đầu, họ sẽ cảm thấy có trách nhiệm và có khả năng chăm sóc không gian hơn.
Ben: Điều đó có lý. Bạn đã nghĩ đến kinh phí chưa? Một dự án như thế này sẽ tốn tiền — dụng cụ, hạt giống, hàng rào, có thể cả đường nước.
Rosa: Tôi đã nghiên cứu các khoản tài trợ dành cho các dự án cộng đồng. Có một khoản từ hội đồng địa phương bao gồm đến hai nghìn đô la cho các không gian xanh. Cũng có một tổ chức từ thiện môi trường quốc gia tài trợ cho các sáng kiến làm vườn đô thị.
Ben: Nhưng hai nghìn đô la có thể chưa đủ. Bạn có nghĩ chúng ta cũng có thể đề nghị các doanh nghiệp địa phương tài trợ không? Tôi biết một vài chủ cửa hàng trên phố chính quan tâm đến khu phố.
Rosa: Ý tưởng hay tuyệt vời. Chúng ta có thể đề nghị đặt tên của họ trên biển hiệu ở lối vào — đó sẽ là quảng bá tốt cho họ và đồng thời giúp chúng ta đạt được ngân sách.
Ben: Còn về thiết kế khu vườn thì sao? Chúng ta có nên có các ô riêng lẻ mà mọi người có thể thuê, hay một không gian trồng trọt chung lớn?
Rosa: Tôi muốn có cả hai. Các ô riêng lẻ cho mọi người một không gian cá nhân để trồng bất cứ thứ gì họ thích, nhưng khu vực chung có nghĩa là chúng ta có thể trồng thực phẩm cho cả cộng đồng, kể cả những người không có thời gian chăm sóc ô riêng của mình.
Ben: Tôi thích ý tưởng đó. Chúng ta thậm chí có thể dành riêng một khu cho trẻ em, với các loại cây trồng dễ chăm như hoa hướng dương và dâu tây. Thu hút trẻ em tham gia từ sớm thực sự rất quan trọng.
Rosa: Tuyệt đối đồng ý. Và nếu chúng ta tổ chức các buổi hội thảo về ủ phân và trồng trọt hữu cơ, chúng ta có thể giáo dục mọi người trong khi xây dựng khu vườn cùng một lúc. Nó trở thành nhiều hơn là chỉ nơi trồng thực phẩm — nó trở thành không gian học tập.
Ben: Điều này thực sự thú vị. Bạn định trình đề xuất lên hội đồng khi nào?
Rosa: Tôi muốn có mọi thứ sẵn sàng trong sáu tuần tới. Nếu chúng ta được chấp thuận đúng thời hạn, chúng ta có thể bắt đầu dọn dẹp đất vào đầu mùa xuân và có khu vườn sẵn sàng để trồng trước mùa hè.
Ben: Tính tôi vào. Tôi sẽ giúp khảo sát cuối tuần này nếu thời tiết vẫn tốt. Hãy cùng biến điều này thành hiện thực.

questions.prompt: What is the current state of the empty lot on Maple Street?
options: It is a paved car park
It is covered in weeds and rubbish
It already has some overgrown plants but no rubbish
It has been fenced off by the council
questions.explanation: Ben says 'It's just sitting there with weeds and rubbish.'
questions.hint: Ben describes what he saw when he walked past.

questions.prompt: What condition has the council set for the project to go ahead?
options: The residents must collect signatures from the whole street
Rosa must raise the full funding before approval
A proper proposal must be submitted
A survey of the land must be completed first
questions.explanation: Rosa says the council is open to the idea 'as long as we present a proper proposal.'
questions.hint: Rosa describes what the council requires before they will say yes.

questions.prompt: What does Rosa want to do before starting any physical work on the garden?
options: Hold a neighbourhood meeting to involve residents from the beginning
Design the layout of the individual plots
Buy all the tools and seeds
questions.explanation: Rosa says 'I'd like to hold a neighbourhood meeting first' so residents feel a sense of ownership.
questions.hint: Rosa believes involving people early increases their commitment.

questions.prompt: What is one of the first practical steps Rosa identifies for the project?
options: Surveying the land to assess its condition and soil quality
Building a fence around the perimeter
questions.explanation: Rosa says 'we need to start by surveying the land to see what condition it's in' and check if the soil is suitable.
questions.hint: Rosa mentions this before they can make decisions about what to grow.

questions.prompt: How much funding is available from the local council grant?
questions.explanation: Rosa says 'There's one from the local council that covers up to two thousand dollars for green spaces.'
questions.hint: Rosa mentions a specific dollar amount when explaining the grant.

questions.prompt: What does Ben suggest to raise additional funds beyond the grants?
options: Charging residents a monthly fee to use the garden
Organising a fundraising event in the neighbourhood
Asking local businesses to sponsor the project
Applying for a national government grant
questions.explanation: Ben suggests 'ask local businesses to sponsor us' since he knows shop owners on the high street.
questions.hint: Ben mentions specific people he knows who might help.

questions.prompt: What benefit would local business sponsors receive in return?
options: A percentage of produce grown in the garden
Their names on a sign at the entrance
Exclusive use of a private plot
questions.explanation: Rosa says 'We could offer to put their names on a sign at the entrance — it would be good publicity for them.'
questions.hint: Rosa describes it as good publicity for the businesses.

questions.prompt: What garden design does Rosa prefer?
options: One large shared growing space for everyone
A mix of individual plots and a shared growing area
A formal garden with flower beds only
questions.explanation: Rosa says 'I'd prefer a mix of both' — individual plots and a shared area.
questions.hint: Rosa explains the advantages of each option before stating her preference.

questions.prompt: What crops does Ben suggest planting in a children's section?
questions.explanation: Ben says 'we could set aside a section for children, with easy crops like sunflowers and strawberries.'
questions.hint: Ben emphasises that these crops are easy to grow.

questions.prompt: What kind of workshops does Rosa plan to run at the garden?
questions.explanation: Rosa says 'if we run workshops on composting and organic growing, we can educate people.'
questions.hint: These workshops relate to sustainable gardening methods.

questions.prompt: When does Rosa hope the garden will be ready to plant?
questions.explanation: Rosa says they could 'have the garden ready to plant before summer' if they get approval in time.
questions.hint: Rosa describes the timeline after getting council approval and clearing the land.

cloze.template:
Rosa: Ben, have you had a chance to look at the empty lot on Maple Street? I've been thinking about it a lot lately.
Ben: Yeah, I walked past it yesterday. It's just sitting there with weeds and rubbish. What are you thinking?
Rosa: I want to turn it into a community garden. I've already spoken to the council, and they're {{b1}} the idea as long as we present a proper proposal.
Ben: That sounds fantastic. I've always wanted to get involved in something like this. Where would we even start?
Rosa: I think we need to start by {{b2}} the land to see what condition it's in. We also need to know if the soil is suitable for growing vegetables, or if we'll need to bring in fresh topsoil.
Ben: Good point. We should probably also think about what kinds of things the community would actually want to grow. Not everyone is interested in vegetables — some people might prefer flowers or {{b3}}.
Rosa: Exactly. That's why I'd like to hold a neighbourhood meeting first. If we get residents involved from the very beginning, they'll feel a {{b4}} and be more likely to take care of the space.
Ben: Have you thought about funding? A project like this is going to cost money — tools, seeds, fencing, maybe a water connection.
Rosa: I've been researching {{b5}} that are available for community projects. There's one from the local council that covers up to two thousand dollars for green spaces.
Ben: Do you think we could also ask local businesses to {{b6}} us? I know a few shop owners on the high street who care about the neighbourhood.
Rosa: That's a brilliant idea. We could offer to put their names on a sign at the entrance — it would be good {{b7}} for them and help us reach our budget at the same time.
Ben: What about the design of the garden itself? Should we have individual plots that people rent, or one big shared growing space?
Rosa: I'd prefer a mix of both. Individual plots give people a personal space, but a shared area means we can grow food for the whole community, including people who don't have time to maintain their own {{b8}}.
Ben: We could even set aside a section for children, with easy crops like sunflowers and strawberries. Getting kids involved early is really important.
Rosa: Absolutely. And if we run workshops on {{b9}} and organic growing, we can educate people while building the garden at the same time.
Ben: When are you thinking of presenting the proposal to the council?
Rosa: I'd like to have everything ready within the next six weeks. If we get approval in time, we could start clearing the land by early spring and have the garden ready to plant before {{b10}}.
Ben: Count me in. I'll help with the survey this weekend if the weather holds.

blanks.explanation: 'Open to the idea' means willing to consider or accept a suggestion — an important fixed phrase in English.
'Surveying the land' is the specific phrase used, meaning to assess a piece of land carefully.
Ben mentions 'flowers or herbs' as alternatives to vegetables — 'herbs' are small aromatic plants used in cooking.
'Sense of ownership' is the phrase used to describe how people feel personally responsible for something they helped create.
'Grants' are sums of money given by organisations for specific purposes, often without needing to be repaid.
'Sponsor' specifically means a business or individual who gives financial support in exchange for recognition or publicity.
'Good publicity' is the phrase used — 'publicity' means attention and public awareness that benefits a business or person.
'Plot' refers to a small piece of land used for growing, particularly in the context of allotments or community gardens.
'Composting' is the process of turning organic waste into fertiliser — the exact word used in Rosa's sentence.
Rosa says the garden should be ready to plant 'before summer', after clearing in early spring.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Rosa believes that involving residents from the beginning creates a stronger community project. Do you agree that community ownership is the key to making shared spaces successful? What are the biggest challenges facing community garden projects, and how would you overcome them?
